phrasal_verbs.meaning: stop doing sth you do regularly
make a player leave a game (eg, football)

A.title: Complete the crossword

E.title: Prepositional phrases (gap fill)
E.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: We were waiting outside the stadium ...... a long time before they finally let us in.

F.title: Word formation
F.instructions: Complete by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

G.title: The need to play (word formation)
G.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line.
G.passage: Why are kittens such (1) ...... (PLAY) animals? They love chasing a ball or a piece of wool, and they always play in a very (2) ...... (ATHLETE) way. But why? All of a kitten's (3) ...... (ACT) when playing are, in fact, important for the future. It might look like (4) ...... (ENTERTAIN), but the kitten is practising its hunting skills. That (5) ...... (HERO) jump onto a toy teaches the kitten a lot. Think about your own (6) ...... (CHILD) and you'll see that you learnt a lot through play.

questions.sentence: (1) (PLAY)
(2) (ATHLETE)
(4) (ENTERTAIN)
(5) (HERO)
(6) (CHILD)

H.title: Word patterns (dialogue)
H.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
H.passage:
Diana: Hello, is that Jenny? I'm bored (1) ...... watching TV and I felt (2) ...... a chat. What are you doing?
Jenny: Hi, Diana. Well, I'm reading a book (3) ...... a Russian writer. It's (4) ...... how to become a great actor.
Diana: Really? Oh, I'm really interested (5) ...... acting. Tell me about it.
Jenny: He says it takes a long time to get good (6) ...... acting. To become popular (7) ...... the public, you need to really understand people.
Diana: That sounds just like me! Tell me more. What else does he say?
